title: Iteration Protocols, Generators, and Lazy Evaluation · Transformation Checkpoint
summary: Process records larger than memory while exposing malformed rows at a controlled boundary. Every item uses a fresh case and misconception-specific distractors. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for a bounded-memory lazy ingestion pipeline. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Trace iter, next, StopIteration, and single-pass iterator state exactly.
Explain generator suspension, local state retention, send points, and termination.
Measure memory and latency tradeoffs between eager and lazy transformation.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.python:
"""python-functional immutable transformation portfolio."""

from collections.abc import Callable, Iterable, Iterator
from functools import partial, reduce
from itertools import chain, islice
from typing import TypeVar

T = TypeVar('T')
U = TypeVar('U')

# Extend one pure, lazy, tested pipeline increment per activity.

steps.title: Iteration Protocol · changed case
steps.instruction:
quiz case — A one-pass sensor iterator yields [6, 9, malformed, 14, 19]. Consume at most 8 records, report malformed input, and never restart or materialize the open-ended source.

Which action demonstrates Iteration Protocol without dropping earlier contracts?
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for trace iter, next, stopiteration, and single-pass iterator state exactly.

content.text: A one-pass sensor iterator yields [6, 9, malformed, 14, 19]. Consume at most 8 records, report malformed input, and never restart or materialize the open-ended source.

Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: Iterable and iterator mean the same reusable object.
